title: Daily Market Digest - July 24, 2026
subtitle: Market attempts recovery amidst tech spending concerns and geopolitical tensions.

items.headline: Stocks Mixed as Tech Struggles Persist; Dow Rebounds while Nasdaq Faces Headwinds
items.summary: U.S. equities are attempting to recover from Thursday's sell-off, with the Dow Jones Industrial Average showing a solid rebound, while the Nasdaq Composite struggles with ongoing concerns surrounding AI infrastructure spending and mixed tech earnings. Geopolitical tensions in the Middle East and new U.S. tariffs are also contributing to a cautious market sentiment. Oil prices have eased slightly today after surging higher on Thursday.

items.statContext: Up +0.59% today; on track for second consecutive weekly loss.
Up +0.09% today; weighed down by tech losses and on track for second consecutive weekly loss.
Up +0.72% today; still set for third consecutive week of declines.

items.headline: Digital Realty Trust Surges on Strong Earnings
items.summary: Digital Realty Trust jumped 15% today, poised for its best day since 2009, following a strong Q2 2026 earnings beat, raised full-year guidance, and analyst upgrades.

items.headline: SLB N.V. Rises on Better-Than-Expected Results
items.summary: SLB N.V. gained 9.7% today after reporting better-than-expected second-quarter financial results and robust international offshore drilling growth.

items.headline: Tenet Healthcare Soars on Earnings Beat
items.summary: Tenet Healthcare soared 14.53% in premarket trading today following a second-quarter earnings beat and a raised full-year profit outlook.

items.headline: Tesla Slumps Amid AI Spending Concerns
items.summary: Tesla's stock dropped significantly on Thursday by 14.5% after reporting weaker-than-expected second-quarter earnings and highlighting surging capital expenditures on AI and robotics projects, which continues to pressure tech valuations today.

items.headline: Intel Falls Despite Upbeat Forecast
items.summary: Intel shares are down 4% today as investors weigh the company's heavy capital spending plans against an otherwise upbeat quarterly revenue and profit forecast.

items.headline: American Express Declines Despite Profit Jump
items.summary: American Express fell 6% today, despite reporting a jump in profit for its most recent quarter, as the company maintained its profit forecast and increased spending to retain wealthy clients amidst rising competition.

items.headline: AI Spending Weighs Heavily on Big Tech Valuations
items.summary: Investor concerns over the growing cost of AI investments are impacting major technology companies. Post-earnings reactions from Alphabet and Tesla highlighted rising capital spending and cash burn, setting a cautious tone for the broader tech sector ahead of upcoming earnings reports.

items.headline: U.S. Imposes New Tariffs on 60 Trading Partners
items.summary: The Trump administration has imposed new tariffs of 10% and 12.5% on goods from 60 trading partners, citing lax enforcement of forced-labor bans. This move follows the expiration of a temporary 10% global tariff and is contributing to broader market uncertainty.

items.headline: Escalating Middle East Tensions Keep Oil Prices Elevated
items.summary: Geopolitical risks in the Middle East are intensifying, with U.S. missiles striking targets across Iran. This conflict continues to fuel concerns about global oil and gas supplies, keeping Brent crude prices hovering above $100 a barrel despite a slight easing today.

items.headline: Q2 Earnings Season Continues with Mixed Results
items.summary: The second-quarter earnings season is progressing with mixed results. While companies like SLB N.V. ($SLB) reported stronger-than-expected figures, major tech players like Intel ($INTC) faced sell-offs due to increased capital expenditure guidance, despite otherwise strong reports. Verizon ($VZ) raised its annual forecasts, while American Express ($AXP) declined post-earnings.

items.headline: U.S. Services Sector Accelerates, Manufacturing Slows
items.summary: Data released today indicated that activity in the U.S. services sector accelerated in July, partly boosted by spending related to the FIFA World Cup and the Independence Day holiday. Conversely, the pace of growth in the manufacturing sector eased to its slowest since March. Initial jobless claims for the week ending July 18 decreased to 187,000.
